title: Comptroller._setCloseFactor lacks upper bound — can be set above 100% enabling protocol-level liquidation abuse
claim: The _setCloseFactor function has no validation that newCloseFactorMantissa <= 1e18. If set above 1e18 (e.g., 2e18 = 200%), the maxClose calculation in liquidateBorrowAllowed (line 3676) would produce a value greater than the borrower's total debt. While repayBorrowFresh caps repayment at the actual borrow balance, the inflated maxClose bypasses the intended partial-liquidation safety mechanism, allowing any liquidator to repay the full borrow in one transaction even for healthy markets. Combined with a stale oracle price, this enables instantaneous full-position liquidations.
impact: If the admin sets closeFactorMantissa > 1e18 (or if the admin key is compromised), liquidators can fully liquidate any position in a single transaction regardless of the intended close factor limit. This removes the partial-liquidation safety mechanism that protects borrowers from full-position wipeout during temporary price fluctuations. Combined with F-004 (stale oracle prices), an attacker could fully liquidate positions that are only temporarily underwater.

paths.description: Admin sets close factor > 100% enabling full liquidations
steps: 1. Admin (or compromised admin) calls _setCloseFactor with value > 1e18
2. In liquidateBorrowAllowed, maxClose = closeFactorMantissa * borrowBalance / 1e18 exceeds borrowBalance
3. The check `if (repayAmount > maxClose)` never triggers for any repayAmount <= borrowBalance
4. Liquidators can now fully liquidate any position, bypassing the partial-close safety design
5. Combined with stale oracle (F-004), positions that are only transiently underwater get fully liquidated

title: Comptroller._setLiquidationIncentive lacks bounds — can be set to 0% or extreme values breaking liquidation economics
claim: The _setLiquidationIncentive function has no bounds check whatsoever. It can be set to 0 (making liquidation unprofitable and breaking the protocol's insolvency-resolution mechanism), or to extremely high values (allowing liquidators to drain >100% of collateral value). Unlike _setCollateralFactor which has an upper bound of 0.9e18, _setLiquidationIncentive is completely unbounded.
impact: If set to 0, no rational actor would perform liquidations, allowing underwater positions to accumulate until the protocol becomes insolvent. If set to a very high value, liquidators can drain excessive collateral from borrowers, effectively stealing funds. On-chain verification shows current value is 1.1e18 (10% incentive), but there is no code preventing a change to 0 or 100e18.

paths.description: Liquidation incentive set to 0 breaks protocol solvency
steps: 1. Admin sets liquidationIncentiveMantissa to 0 via _setLiquidationIncentive
2. In liquidateCalculateSeizeTokens, numerator = 0 * priceBorrowed = 0
3. seizeTokens = 0 — liquidators receive no collateral for repaying debt
4. No rational liquidator will liquidate positions; underwater positions accumulate
5. Protocol accrues bad debt until totalBorrows > totalCash + totalReserves, becoming insolvent

paths.description: Liquidation incentive set extremely high enables collateral drain
steps: 1. Admin sets liquidationIncentiveMantissa to 10e18 (1000%)
2. Liquidators can seize collateral worth 10x the debt they repay
3. Borrowers lose far more collateral than economically sensible
4. Protocol reserve accumulation (protocolSeizeShare) is also inflated

title: sweepToken has no access control — anyone can trigger token sweeps to admin
claim: The sweepToken function is declared `external` with no access modifier (no onlyAdmin, no onlyOwner, no require). Any external caller can sweep any ERC20 token (except the underlying) from the OToken contract to the admin address. While the funds go to admin rather than the caller, this enables griefing attacks where a third party monitors the mempool for accidental token transfers into the OToken contract and front-runs the rightful owner's recovery attempt.
impact: An attacker can monitor the OToken contract for accidental token transfers. When a user mistakenly sends valuable tokens to the OToken address, the attacker can immediately call sweepToken to move those tokens to the admin, permanently preventing the original sender from recovering them (recovery would then depend on admin cooperation). This is a griefing vector that exploits the lack of access control.

steps: 1. Attacker monitors mempool for ERC20 transfers to the OToken contract address
2. When a transfer (other than the underlying token) is detected, attacker submits sweepToken transaction with higher gas
3. Tokens are swept to admin before the original sender can attempt recovery
4. Original sender permanently loses tokens unless admin voluntarily returns them

title: decimals() == 0 used as NFT detection — 0-decimal ERC20 tokens would be misidentified causing decimal scaling errors in pricing and liquidation
claim: Throughout the protocol, `oToken.decimals() == 0` is used as a heuristic to detect NFT (ERC721) markets. In the oracle (line 3100), this triggers an extra `price.mul(10**18)` multiplication. In liquidation calculations (line 3992 and 2283), it triggers `extendingDecimal = 1e18` and routes to the NFT-specific `liquidateCalculateSeizeTokensEx`. However, ERC20 tokens can legitimately have 0 decimals (e.g., some stablecoins on certain chains). If a 0-decimal ERC20 token were used as underlying, the protocol would silently treat it as an NFT, applying incorrect decimal scaling that breaks all price-dependent calculations.
impact: If a market is ever created with a 0-decimal ERC20 underlying (e.g., GUSD on some chains or certain legacy tokens), the oracle would return prices inflated by 1e18, making collateral appear astronomically valuable. Borrowers could drain all protocol liquidity against dust collateral. Conversely, the liquidity calculation would be inconsistent with the liquidation calculation, creating unpredictable liquidation behavior.

paths.description: 0-decimal ERC20 token misidentified as NFT causes protocol insolvency
steps: 1. Admin deploys a market with a legitimate 0-decimal ERC20 token as underlying
2. ChainlinkOracle.getPrice detects oToken.decimals() == 0 and applies price.mul(10**18)
3. Oracle returns price inflated by 1e18 (e.g., $1 token priced at $1e18)
4. Borrowers can mint oTokens with $1 of underlying and borrow against $1e18 of collateral value
5. Protocol liquidity is completely drained; bad debt accumulates

title: maxAssets state variable declared but never enforced — accounts can enter unlimited markets causing gas-based DoS risk for liquidation
claim: The ComptrollerV1Storage declares `uint public maxAssets` with documentation stating the accountAssets mapping is 'capped by maxAssets'. However, the addToMarketInternal function (line 3289) contains no check against maxAssets. An account can call enterMarkets repeatedly to add an unlimited number of markets. The getHypotheticalAccountLiquidityInternal function iterates over all entered markets, and liquidation calls this function. A malicious actor could enter hundreds of markets specifically to make their own liquidation prohibitively gas-expensive, creating a griefing-based liquidation avoidance mechanism.
impact: A borrower who is at risk of liquidation could enter a large number of markets to inflate the gas cost of getHypotheticalAccountLiquidityInternal, potentially making liquidation transactions exceed the block gas limit. This is a form of gas-based DoS attack on liquidators. While the attacker also pays gas to enter markets, the one-time cost is low compared to the benefit of avoiding liquidation.

steps: 1. Borrower's position approaches liquidation threshold
2. Borrower calls enterMarkets with a large array of listed markets (potentially deployed by the attacker at low cost)
3. getHypotheticalAccountLiquidityInternal now iterates over many markets, increasing gas cost
4. Liquidation transactions may exceed block gas limit or become uneconomical for liquidators
5. Borrower avoids liquidation despite being underwater

title: Comptroller._setCloseFactor lacks lower bound — can be set to 0 completely disabling all liquidations for non-deprecated non-NFT markets
claim: The _setCloseFactor function has zero bounds validation. While F-005 covers the upper bound (close factor > 100% allowing full liquidations), the lower bound is equally destructive. Setting closeFactorMantissa to 0 makes maxClose = mul_ScalarTruncate(Exp({mantissa: 0}), borrowBalance) = 0 in liquidateBorrowAllowed. Since repayAmount > 0 is enforced by liquidateBorrowFresh, the check `repayAmount > maxClose` ALWAYS triggers, returning TOO_MUCH_REPAY for EVERY liquidation attempt on non-deprecated, non-NFT markets. The only escape hatches are: (a) deprecated markets (which bypass the close factor check entirely), and (b) NFT liquidations where extraRepayAmount > 0 bypasses the check.
impact: Setting closeFactorMantissa to 0 permanently bricks the liquidation mechanism for all normal (non-deprecated, non-NFT) markets. Underwater positions cannot be liquidated, bad debt accumulates, and the protocol becomes insolvent as totalBorrows grows beyond totalCash + totalReserves. Combined with F-004 (stale oracle prices that may cause false shortfalls), the protocol would be unable to resolve any underwater positions, making the bad debt permanent. Unlike the upper bound issue which only removes partial-liquidation protection, the lower bound issue completely prevents ANY liquidation from succeeding.

paths.description: Close factor set to 0 bricks all liquidations
steps: 1. Admin (or compromised admin) calls _setCloseFactor(0)
2. In liquidateBorrowAllowed, maxClose = mul_ScalarTruncate(Exp({mantissa: 0}), borrowBalance) = 0
3. For any non-zero repayAmount, repayAmount > 0 = true, so TOO_MUCH_REPAY is returned
4. All liquidateBorrow calls on non-deprecated markets fail regardless of how underwater the position is
5. Bad debt accumulates as borrowers cannot be liquidated; protocol trends toward insolvency
6. Only deprecated markets (collateralFactor=0, borrowGuardianPaused=true, reserveFactor=1e18) or NFT markets (extraRepayAmount>0) remain liquidatable

optimalParameters.closeFactorMantissa: 0 (or any value where maxClose truncates to 0, i.e., < 1e18/borrowBalance)
optimalParameters.note: The attack is trivially executed by setting the parameter to 0; no complex parameter search required

paths.description: Contrast with _setCollateralFactor which has proper bounds
steps: _setCollateralFactor has upper bound 0.9e18 via lessThanExp(highLimit, newCollateralFactorExp)
_setReserveFactor has upper bound 1e18 via newReserveFactorMantissa > reserveFactorMaxMantissa
_setCloseFactor and _setLiquidationIncentive are the only economic parameters without ANY bounds
This is a systematic failure of input validation on protocol-critical economic parameters

title: ChainlinkOracle.setAdmin lacks zero-address validation — can permanently brick oracle management by transferring admin to address(0)
claim: The ChainlinkOracle.setAdmin function has no check preventing newAdmin from being address(0). If set to zero, the onlyAdmin modifier on all administrative functions (setFeed, setUnderlyingPrice, setDirectPrice, setAdmin) would permanently reject all calls since msg.sender can never equal address(0). While the Comptroller can switch to a new oracle via _setPriceOracle, the transition period leaves protocol operating with frozen oracle settings — no new Chainlink feeds can be registered and no manual price overrides can be applied.
impact: An operational error or compromised admin could permanently brick the ChainlinkOracle by setting admin to address(0). While existing Chainlink feeds continue to return prices (they are read directly from Chainlink, not from oracle storage), the protocol loses the ability to: (1) add price feeds for new markets, (2) set manual price overrides for assets without Chainlink feeds, (3) correct misconfigured feeds. The Comptroller admin would need to deploy a new oracle and call _setPriceOracle to recover, during which time new market listings are blocked and mispriced assets cannot be corrected.

paths.description: Accidental or malicious admin zero-out bricks oracle
steps: 1. Current oracle admin calls setAdmin(address(0)) — either accidentally or maliciously
2. All onlyAdmin-guarded functions (setFeed, setUnderlyingPrice, setDirectPrice, setAdmin) become permanently inaccessible
3. New markets requiring new Chainlink feeds cannot be safely listed
4. Any asset using setDirectPrice or setUnderlyingPrice is frozen at its last-set value
5. Comptroller admin must deploy replacement oracle and call _setPriceOracle to recover
6. During transition, affected markets operate with potentially stale or incorrect prices
